U
UNI-CHAT
Problem Corpus Architecture Tour Conclusion Team
Read the project
Hacettepe University · Senior Project

UNI-CHAT — an AI assistant for Turkish academic regulations.

A graph-based retrieval system that answers regulation questions with source-grounded citations and a hierarchical norm-resolution layer modeled on how legal experts reason.

Read the project Explore architecture
162
Documents
across 3 authorities
500
Senate decisions
14-year span
66
Petitions
official templates
4
Retrieval modes
Naive · Local · Global · Hybrid
01 — Problem statement

Academic regulations are scattered across three hierarchical levels.

National laws (YÖK), university senate decisions, and faculty guidelines all overlap. Students, faculty, and administrative staff must navigate 162 documents, 66 petition templates, and 500 senate decisions to find accurate answers.

Existing search tools and general-purpose LLMs fail to capture this structure — leading to:

  • Missed deadlines
  • Incorrect petitions
  • Legally inconsistent guidance
162
Documents
Departmental, university, and YÖK regulations.
500
Senate decisions
Weekly minutes that amend or override prior rules.
66
Petition templates
Official forms, each tied to its own filing rules.
02 — Solution

A graph that understands the law.

UNI-CHAT is built on LightRAG, a graph-augmented retrieval framework. We extended it with a domain-specific norm-resolution layer for Turkish academic regulations.

01 / 03

Knowledge graph

Entities and relations are extracted from every regulation, building a queryable graph that captures how rules reference, override, and depend on each other.

02 / 03

Hierarchical norm resolution

When two rules conflict, the system applies lex specialis — the more specific lower-level norm wins, mirroring how legal experts reason.

03 / 03

Source-grounded answers

Every response is backed by the exact document and chunk it came from. No hallucinations, no untraceable claims.

Normative hierarchy

Lower-scope norms override broader regulations on specific cases.

L3
Council of Higher Education (YÖK)
National regulations · broadest scope
4 docs
overridden by
L2
Hacettepe University
University-wide regulations & senate decisions
158 docs
overridden by
L1
Department-specific guidelines
Most specific · CS & AI Engineering
~23 docs
03 — The corpus

What the system actually reads.

The knowledge base blends three tiers of regulations with 14 years of senate minutes and the official petition catalogue — every source tracked, hashed, and re-indexed on change.

#
Source
Authority
Count
Tier
01
Department guidelines
Internships, curriculum, graduation, research groups, FAQs.
CS & AI Engineering
Internships, curriculum, graduation, research groups, FAQs.
23
documents

02
University regulations
Academic, disciplinary, administrative, transfer & exemption rules.

Academic, disciplinary, administrative, transfer & exemption rules.
135
documents

National regulations
Graduate education, joint programs, research-assistant exchange.
Council of Higher Education (YÖK)
Graduate education, joint programs, research-assistant exchange.

documents

Senate decisions

documents

Petition templates
Official forms, browsable in-app, exportable as DOCX.

documents
P
Σ
Total — corpus size
Indexed into 3,068 retrievable chunks
728
docs
Senate decisions, 2012 – 2026

Fourteen years of institutional change, week by week.

Each weekly senate session can amend or supersede earlier rules. We index every published decision and surface the governing one during retrieval.

26

Peak year · 2015 (52)
14-year span
Σ 495 indexed decisions
04 — Methodology pipeline

Indexed once. Answered fast.

A dual-LLM design separates costly graph construction from latency-critical query answering. Documents are indexed once; queries hit a fast retrieval pipeline.

Ingestion
Run once · builds the graph
Step 01

Data collection

Scrape & curate 162 regulations and 500 senate decisions across 3 authorities.

Step 02

Text preprocessing

Normalize Turkish text, segment into 3,068 chunks with metadata.

Step 03

Knowledge graph

Extract entities & relations with an LLM into a queryable graph.

indexed corpus → live queries
Query
Per question · latency optimized
Step 04

Keyword extraction

Parse user query into low- and high-level search keywords.

Step 05

Multi-modal retrieval

Naive, Local, Global, and Hybrid retrieval run in parallel.

Step 06

Reranking

Cross-encoder reranks candidates for relevance.

Step 07

Norm resolution

Apply hierarchical lex specialis rule to resolve conflicts.

Step 08

Answer generation

A second LLM synthesizes a grounded answer in Turkish.

Step 09

Output + sources

Return the answer with inline citations to source chunks.

05 — Capabilities

Five retrieval modes. One unified interface.

Different questions need different retrieval strategies. UNI-CHAT exposes all five — plus the engineering work that makes a real production system.

Mode 01

Naive

Vector similarity over raw chunks — the baseline.

Best for
Quick keyword lookups
Mode 02

Local

Entity-relationship retrieval focused on specific concepts.

Best for
Targeted "what does X mean?" questions
Mode 03

Global

High-level summaries built from graph communities.

Best for
Overview queries about a regulation area
Mode 04

Hybrid

Local + Global — combined for the best dual-context answer.

Best for
Default — works for almost everything
Mode 05

Mix

Naive + Local + Global signals fused into a single response.

Best for
Hard, multi-faceted questions
Engineering details

What it takes to ship.

01
66 templates

Petition system

Browse 66 official Hacettepe petition templates. Inline PDF preview powered by LibreOffice; download as editable DOCX.

02
4 providers

Dual-LLM architecture

Independent providers for graph construction (cost-optimized) and chat (quality-optimized). Switch between Ollama, OpenAI, Gemini, DeepSeek without rebuilding.

03
O(Δ) updates

Incremental ingestion

Hash-based change detection — only modified documents are re-processed. Add new regulations without rebuilding the entire graph.

04
Multi-tenant

Workspace isolation

Multiple knowledge bases coexist on a single deployment. Each department or faculty can maintain its own graph.

06 — Product tour

See it in practice.

A graph-based RAG system isn't an abstract diagram — it's a tool people actually use. Here is what UNI-CHAT looks like end-to-end, from the chat that answers a regulation question to the panels that manage the corpus behind it.

Step 01 · Retrieval

Ask in Turkish — get a sourced answer

Questions are asked in plain Turkish. Every answer is bound to the exact source chunks it was drawn from — each claim shows the originating PDF and section reference. No hallucinations: every fact is traceable.

Step 02 · Knowledge graph

The corpus as a navigable map

Entities and relations extracted from 162 documents form a queryable knowledge graph. Each node is a concept (a regulation, a course, an authority); each edge is a reference, override, or dependency. Lookup, filter, and navigate the same structure the retrieval pipeline uses.

Step 03 · Documents

Every source, hash-tracked

Every document carries an upload time, a content hash, and a status. When a regulation changes, only its chunks are re-processed — the rest of the graph stays intact. The pipeline reports completed, processing, pending, and failed states per file.

Step 04 · Senate decisions

14 years of weekly senate minutes

Every published senate decision since 2012 is browsable, searchable by date, and previewable in-app. When a recent decision overrides an older regulation, the system surfaces the governing one during retrieval — not just the most relevant one.

Step 05 · Petitions

Browse, preview, download — 66 official forms

The full Hacettepe petition catalogue is accessible from the same interface. Filter by category (Akademik, Askerlik, Lisansüstü, Staj, Sınav, …), preview the form inline, then export a ready-to-fill DOCX. No more hunting across faculty pages.

07 — Conclusion

Why graph-based RAG wins.

The proposed system effectively retrieves and synthesizes Turkish academic regulations across three hierarchical levels using graph-based RAG. The dual-LLM architecture balances cost, latency, and quality, while hierarchical norm resolution ensures legally consistent responses. Results confirm that domain-specific knowledge-graph construction substantially outperforms naive vector search for structured regulatory corpora.

Finding 01

Domain-specific knowledge graphs win on structured corpora

Across the LightRAG benchmark suite, graph-augmented retrieval substantially outperforms naive vector search on regulatory and legal text — the closest analogs to our corpus.

Finding 02

Hierarchical norm resolution yields legally consistent answers

Encoding lex specialis directly into the retrieval layer ensures that the more specific lower-level norm is preferred when rules conflict.

Finding 03

A dual-LLM split balances cost, latency, and quality

Costly graph construction is decoupled from latency-critical query answering — each LLM is chosen for the constraint that matters most at that stage.

Appendix — Stack

Built with production tools.

Every piece is something you'd ship in industry — chosen for stability, observability, and operational ease.

Backend 01
  • 01 Python 3.11
  • 02 FastAPI
  • 03 LightRAG
  • 04 NetworkX
  • 05 asyncio
AI / LLM 02
  • 01 Ollama
  • 02 OpenAI
  • 03 Gemini
  • 04 DeepSeek
  • 05 BGE Reranker
Frontend 03
  • 01 React 19
  • 02 TypeScript
  • 03 Vite
  • 04 Tailwind CSS
  • 05 i18next
Infrastructure 04
  • 01 Docker
  • 02 Nginx
  • 03 AWS EC2
  • 04 LibreOffice
  • 05 Bun
